transcript.clean_text: Stage six of the Dauphine, destination Gap after 196.5 km from Rive. Beautiful weather, hovering around 26-27 degrees Celsius, and a tailwind for much of the day, and that meant that a break had, once again, a great chance of making it all the way. So, the battles were intense at the beginning. Last chance for the quick men, perhaps, but they'd have to work hard for it. With the Alps looming, how much support would they get with riders and teams preserving themselves for the great GC battle still to come. Wout van Aert earning the leader's jersey, leading the race by just over a minute. Wears the points jersey, as well, but it was mountain points that Pierre Rolland was interested in. He got himself into what was to be an extremely powerful breakaway. Victor Lafay of Cofidis was up there, the pre-stage favorite for the day. Rolland doing a good job of extending his lead in the polka dot competition. Valentin Ferron of Total Energies was there. Likewise, Bagioli, the quickest man of the break from Quick-Step Alpha Vinyl. Warren Barguil, great French rider, was there with Geoffrey Bouchard, and early on, Bruno Armirail. Well, they started to exchange duties beautifully. It was a team time trialing unit, and the chase just couldn't decide how it was going to be assembled. First, Trek-Segafredo had had a go, then up had come a number of other teams, including UAE, Intermarché-Wanty, Groupama-FDJ after Armirail dropped out of the break, but they really could not organize themselves, and tempers started to fray. Well, it'd be one for the commissaires, but say a blow to the head of a an opponent usually means an exclusion. Silly stuff. The breakaway had a handsome lead by the time we got to the tipping point, a little climb over the crest of which would be downhill all the way into Gap, coming at 4.6 km out. And once they started their descent, there was surely going to be no catching them. And indeed, that was the case. By the time we got to the flamme rouge, they were just waiting to see what Bagioli would do. In fact, everybody was. But one man had an idea that if you were going to beat Bagioli, you had to go early. And Valentin Ferron got the jump. Nobody was prepared to chase. They all sat on Bagioli's wheel. He was the main threat to them all on the day, and Bagioli was going to be nobody's fool. He started to cat and mouse, while Valentin Ferron just pushed on for the line. Came to the last 150-m ramp, and then everyone woke up to the chance. Pierre Rolland went for it, so did Warren Barguil, but Ferron took the day. A second great win for Total Energies after Vuillermoz had won from a break earlier in the race on stage two. Minor prizes up for grabs for the peloton who crossed the line in arrears. Over half a minute down, Molano leading Buchmann, Hagen, Groenewegen, and Louvel, but Valentin Ferron getting the biggest win of his career. Just turned 24, maybe it's the start of many. Certainly an amazing day for him, and a great race this has been for Team Total Energies with two stage wins. Absolutely delightful. Well, the emotions came to the fore, and there'll be plenty more of those as we head for the Alps. Wout van Aert leads the race by that same margin as he started the day, over a minute and three over Catania. Roglic and Vingegaard will be the focus, I imagine, for his team, but you can never be sure what this man can produce. Let's see how he goes as tomorrow we come out of the back door with the Galibier. We have the Col du Lautaret. No outdoers in this reflective stage of the Tour de France as we head towards Vaujany, but it is going to be fascinating. Look at that for a profile. Two or category climbs. This race is mean. Tomorrow, it's going to be royally entertaining, as it always is. We'll see you then.
